attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-day history of increased cough, purulent sputum production and mild dyspnoea. She has a medical history of bronchiectasis with Pseudomonas aeruginosa isolated from sputum cultures six months ago. She takes perindopril and performs daily chest physiotherapy.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 82/min, blood pressure 138/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. Chest examination reveals coarse crackles at the left base.

An ECG performed for routine monitoring shows sinus rhythm with a QTc interval of 550 ms (350-440). Investigations reveal a white cell count of 12.2 × 10⁹/L (4.0-11.0), C-reactive protein 42 mg/L (<5), sodium 140 mmol/L (135-145), potassium 4.2 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), creatinine 85 μmol/L (60-110), and computed tomography pulmonary angiogram shows left lower lobe bronchiectatic changes with mucus plugging and no pulmonary embolism.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate antibiotic therapy?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with an acute exacerbation of bronchiectasis. The history of Pseudomonas aeruginosa isolation and the significantly prolonged QTc interval are crucial factors in antibiotic selection. The prolonged QTc of 550 ms represents a significant risk factor for torsades de pointes, particularly with medications that further prolong the QT interval.

Piperacillin-tazobactam is the most appropriate choice as it provides excellent antipseudomonal coverage without affecting the QT interval. It is a beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combination that is first-line for bronchiectasis exacerbations with known or suspected Pseudomonas infection.

Ciprofloxacin, while providing good antipseudomonal coverage, is contraindicated in this patient due to the prolonged QTc. Fluoroquinolones are known to prolong the QT interval and increase the risk of potentially fatal arrhythmias, particularly torsades de pointes. Importantly, ciprofloxacin is the only commonly available oral antibiotic with antipseudomonal activity, and when it is contraindicated, intravenous antibiotics are likely needed for exacerbations in patients with known Pseudomonas.

Amoxicillin-clavulanate lacks antipseudomonal activity and would be inadequate for this patient with previous Pseudomonas isolation. It is appropriate for mild bronchiectasis exacerbations without Pseudomonas risk factors.

Doxycycline also lacks antipseudomonal coverage and is generally reserved for patients with mild exacerbations or those with contraindications to first-line agents. It would not be appropriate given the previous Pseudomonas isolation.

Azithromycin, a macrolide antibiotic, can also prolong the QT interval and lacks reliable antipseudomonal activity. While it has anti-inflammatory properties beneficial in bronchiectasis, it would not be appropriate as monotherapy in this case.

The management of bronchiectasis exacerbations requires consideration of previous microbiology results, severity of presentation, and patient-specific contraindications. In this case, the combination of Pseudomonas risk and QTc prolongation significantly narrows the therapeutic options, making piperacillin-tazobactam the clear choice.
learningPoints: Fluoroquinolones should be avoided in patients with prolonged QTc interval due to risk of torsades de pointes.
Previous isolation of Pseudomonas aeruginosa in bronchiectasis requires antipseudomonal antibiotic coverage during exacerbations.
When oral antipseudomonal therapy is contraindicated, intravenous antibiotics are typically required for bronchiectasis exacerbations.
references: Chang AB, Bell SC, Byrnes CA, Dawkins P, Holland AE, Kennedy E, et al. Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand (TSANZ) position statement on chronic suppurative lung disease and bronchiectasis in children, adolescents and adults in Australia and New Zealand. Respirol Carlton Vic. 2023 Apr;28(4):339–49.
Hill A.T., Sullivan A.L., Chalmers J.D., De Soyza A., Stuart Elborn J., Andres Floto R., et al. British thoracic society guideline for bronchiectasis in adults. Thorax. 2019;74(Supplement 1):74.
Polverino E, Goeminne PC, McDonnell MJ, Aliberti S, Marshall SE, Loebinger MR, et al. European Respiratory Society guidelines for the management of adult bronchiectasis. Eur Respir J. 2017;50(3).
